panels.title: Request rate
panels.description: Requests per second across all models. Stacked by status. The error fraction surfaces backend issues vs validation issues separately.

panels.title: Tokens generated per second
panels.description: The right load metric for LLM workloads. A high token rate at low request rate means long generations; a high request rate at low token rate means short outputs. Cost correlates with this, not request count.

panels.title: Latency p50 / p95 / p99
panels.description: Three percentiles cover most operational decisions: p50 = typical user, p95 = users who notice and complain, p99 = tail experience that drives support tickets. Most teams over-watch p50 and under-watch p99.

panels.title: Latency distribution
panels.description: Heatmap over the histogram bucket edges from chapter 6.6.4 (0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 30, 60s). Bimodal LLM latency is a feature: a fast cluster (cached / short outputs) and a slow cluster (long generations) at opposite ends.

panels.title: Backend healthy
panels.description: 1 if the backend's /health probe succeeded on the most recent check; 0 otherwise. A healthy backend serving 30-second p99 latencies is a problem (see latency panel) -- but a different problem from a backend that's down.

panels.title: Utilization
panels.description: Active inference time as a fraction of wall-clock uptime. The chapter's central cost insight: self-hosted billing rewards keeping the GPU hot. 100% means 'every wall-clock second was spent on inference.' Persistent low utilization means you're paying for idle silicon -- consider downsizing or batching.

panels.title: Spend so far
panels.description: Cumulative cost since the control plane started, computed from uptime times the deployment's effective rate. In metered mode you pay this whether or not the GPU is busy; in reserved/owned modes the underlying cost is fixed and this is informational.

panels.title: Active inference time
panels.description: Cumulative seconds spent serving inference requests. Combined with uptime, gives the utilization metric above.

panels.title: Cross-provider monthly cost projection (at current utilization)
panels.description: Given the current rate of active inference, what would a month look like on each provider in providers.yaml? Cheapest entry is the right pick for the *workload* (active rate), even if it isn't the right pick for the *peak* (which the latency panel speaks to). Per-provider rates are static; the projection scales linearly with active-seconds rate. The chapter's cost-economics narrative is built around reading this panel against the latency one.
